Final Project

Emily Arbieto

2025-04-19

Introduction

Hello, My name is Emily Arbieto and I am a 3rd Year Statistics Major studying at the university of Virginia. Additionally, I am pursuing a political science minor, where we focus on regime type and several influencing factors like economic condition, backsliding, corruption, literacy, etc. One of the more difficult topics of Political Science is that we usually run into the problem of causality- we can’t usually figure out if many factors cause another when it comes to regime type, and so today I wanted to focus on geography. This project explores how geographic and political factors shape the distribution and dimensions of freedom around the world. Through a series of interactive and static visualizations, we examine how population, landlocked status, regime type, and region relate to measures of political rights, civil liberties, and rule of law. I aim to challenge assumptions about geography as destiny and highlight the uneven progress of freedom globally.

Visualization 1

This tree map illustrates the global distribution of freedom across different continents, highlighting significant regional disparities. Much of the world is still “not free.” Notably, Africa and the Middle East are marked by a larger proportion of Not Free countries, reflecting the challenges these regions face in establishing and maintaining democratic systems. In contrast, Europe and the Americas show a predominance of Free nations, underscoring the political stability and democratic governance in these areas. By using population size as a basis for the area of each section, the tree map allows viewers to visually grasp how freedom status is distributed relative to global populations. The clear color coding and simple design principles of this visualization make it an accessible tool for exploring the intersection of geography and regime type, reinforcing the idea that political freedom is not uniform across the globe and challenging assumptions about geography determining a nation’s political future.

Visualization 2

In this 2nd visualization, we explore to see if another aspect of geography, being landlocked, has any effect on regime. It challenges the assumption that geography alone dictates a country’s political trajectory. The lines linking landlocked countries don’t all point to being not free, although there are very few countries that are actually free. Conversely, Not Landlocked countries (represented in blue) are more likely to be Free (F), suggesting that access to the sea and international trade may contribute to more favorable political and economic conditions condusive to democracy. A small thing to note is that the Middle east, as seen in the previous visualization, has no free countries, as well as having no landlocked countries. This also suggests that there could be larger factors at play, as the middle east has historically been a hot spot for armed conflict, oil exports (economic disparities), religious influence- much of which is hard to pinpoint for causality.

Visualization 3

Africa stands out, with many landlocked countries falling into the Not Free (NF) or Partly Free (PF) categories. One thing to note is that it almost follows a North South trend, where the northern states are more “Not Free” over time and turbulent, though the southernmost country, Botswana goes on a firmly “more free” journey over time. Over the more recent years (post 2000s) many countries become turbulent from a once steady stream towards a more democratic regime. This could possibly reflect democratic backsliding and less trust in the government.

Eurasia and Asia also have significant portions of landlocked countries, with some of them categorized as Partly Free (PF), which might reflect the transitional nature of governance in these regions. Though there is actually a spectacle- Mongolia was able to transition despite being in between 2 world superpowers known for being an authoritarian/not free regime. Mongolia had been a Soviet satellite state for much of the 20th century, aligning itself closely with the Soviet Union politically, economically, and militarily. In the late 1980s, it transitioned (abruptly) into an Electoral democracy. This followed the collapse of the Soviet Union.

The landlocked countries in Europe present an interesting case when examining the evolution of political regimes over time. Unlike many landlocked countries in the other regions, the landlocked nations of Europe have generally followed a path toward democracy and stability, especially past the World War 2 Era. The geopolitical environment in Europe plays a major role in shaping the political stability of landlocked nations. Many European countries, including landlocked ones, are part of the European Union (EU), which promotes democratic values, rule of law, and human rights. The presence of neighboring liberal democracies and the economic and political stability provided by the EU has allowed landlocked European nations to thrive democratically.

Visualization 4

This line plot shows the counts of each regime type over time. The elephant in the room comes from closed autocracies declining over time. This greatly contrasts from the idea that our first plot drives- which is that most of the world is under a not-free or autocratic regime. Overall, they have been declining- especially around the late 1900s. Here, we see a sharp decline of Closed Autocracies where Liberal and Electoral democracies see the most growth. Some instances could include the fall of the Berlin Wall (1989) and the end of apartheid in South Africa (1994) marked significant milestones in this global trend toward more open, democratic governance. Around the 2000s, the number of Electoral democracies began to increase slightly, but the Liberal democracies showed only moderate growth and even decline. At the same time, Electoral autocracies surged, indicating a global trend of democratic backsliding. This period saw hybrid regimes where countries held elections, but the fairness and openness of those elections were increasingly in doubt. Countries in regions like Eastern Europe, Africa, and Asia experienced a mix of electoral reforms but continued to suffer from restrictions on civil liberties and political rights.

Visualization 5

In this third visualization, I examine the variation in political rights and civil liberties across different regions, emphasizing that geography does indeed play a role in political systems but is not the sole determinant. The boxplots clearly show that while continents like Africa and the Middle East generally score higher in political rights and civil liberties (meaning lower levels of freedom), there is considerable variation within each region. For example, Asia has a wide range of scores, indicating that political freedoms and civil liberties can be both very high and very low within the same geographical region. Eurasia and Europe also show considerable variation, with Europe generally being more free (with lower scores on both axes), but even here, countries with more restrictive regimes still exist. This variation challenges the idea that geography is the sole factor influencing political freedoms. Rather, the data suggests that while geographic region may provide some context for political systems, internal factors like regime type, historical context, and external pressures (such as economic conditions and international relations) play a larger role. The wide variation within regions like Asia and Eurasia further supports the idea that political trajectories are not constrained by geographic boundaries

Visualization 6

In this visualization, I wanted to explore the relationship between population across different continents and Freedom- are most countries of similar population within continents of the same status? The largest countries in terms of population, such as China, India, and United States, are visually prominent. China and India, despite their large populations, are shown with a larger portion of the country marked as Not Free (NF) or Partly Free (PF), highlighting how even populous countries can struggle with democratic freedoms- which is against the general assumption that democracy would flourish in larger spaces. China is particularly notable for being a closed autocracy, despite its massive population. Russia, China, and the United States are all notably large Countries, though they have such different outcomes. In the zoomed-in view of smaller countries, we can observe that even among smaller nations (those with populations under 5 million), there is significant variation in freedom status. For example, countries like Panama, Uruguay, and Mongolia show up as Free (F), with larger blue boxes, indicating that even small nations can have stable democratic systems. Maybe this would suggest that population is actually a stronger indicator at the micro level, though on the larger scale, most continents have a consistent pattern, where most of 1 continent will have a consistent regime. This visualization also furthers a question of population density, and if there is a relationship with regime.

Visualization 7

https://emmiya.shinyapps.io/STATFINALPROJ/

This Shiny app visualization could be used to explore how rule of law factors intersect with a country’s freedom status, providing deeper insight into the underlying conditions that contribute to a country’s political trajectory. The radar chart highlights critical factors such as corruption, government accountability, and civil justice, which are key to understanding whether a country leans towards authoritarianism or democracy. Much of these factors are key in influencing democratic backsliding, predicting when a country may go into a different regime. By comparing these factors with the freedom status data in the tree maps and box plots, we can examine how governance issues like lack of accountability and weak legal frameworks often correlate with low freedom scores. For instance, regions or countries that perform poorly on civil justice or regulatory enforcement may also show high proportions of Not Free (NF) nations, reflecting how poor governance can lead to restricted political rights and civil liberties. This app adds a layer of granular analysis to the broader trends in regime type and freedom status, helping to illustrate that geopolitical influences, corruption, and governance are major factors in determining political freedoms, beyond just geographic location or population size.

Visualization 8

https://emmiya.shinyapps.io/STATFINALPROJ2/

This Shiny app visualization offers a comprehensive look at how constraints on government powers relate to a country’s freedom status across continents. The bar plot shows that countries with high constraints on government powers, such as New Zealand, Canada, and Germany, are classified as Free (F), reflecting strong democratic systems and high levels of political freedoms. In contrast, countries with low constraints, like Russia, Iran, and Afghanistan, are marked as Not Free (NF), illustrating authoritarian regimes with significant restrictions on civil liberties and political rights. The data table on the left furthers this visual analysis by providing a detailed list of countries with their corresponding scores, allowing users to explore the exact values for constraints on government powers and compare them across regions and years. This interaction highlights the variability within regions, where even countries in the same continent can exhibit vastly different political environments.

Conclusion

This series of visualizations collectively underscore the complex relationship between geography, regime type, and freedom status. While geography plays a role in shaping political environments, the data clearly illustrates that regime type is not solely determined by a country’s location or population size. The treemaps and alluvial diagrams reveal that countries across different continents, regardless of their geographic proximity, can exhibit a wide range of political regimes—from democratic to authoritarian—challenging the assumption that geographic factors alone predict political freedom.

For instance, landlocked countries like Switzerland and Botswana can experience democratic governance, while others like Mali and Afghanistan face significant governance challenges. Similarly, the Shiny app visualizations show how rule of law factors, such as constraints on government powers, significantly influence the freedom status of countries. Countries with strong democratic institutions, like New Zealand and Canada, score high on government constraints, while those with authoritarian regimes, like Russia and Iran, have low constraints and are classified as Not Free.

The variation within regions, such as the differences in constraints across Asia or Africa, further emphasizes that internal governance factors, including historical context, corruption, political culture, and institutional development, play a far more significant role in determining political freedom than geography alone. Many of which hold interesting events as to why they switch over. I hope that the visualizations can pique interest in these different stories.

Much of my project was meant to be in a colorblind friendly palette as well as being able to be seen without the use of a keyboard- barring the search bar.
